summary: When restoring teeth with significant gaps using amalgam, place multiple sequential wedges instead of single larger pieces; doing so allows better control and sealing at the margin compared to relying solely on one wedge or no additional aids during restoration procedures involving substantial interproximal defects. Agent2 reasoned that when dealing with a wide gap necessitating meticulous alignment—such as seen in molars restored via amalgams—the appropriate method involves inserting several smaller wedges successively into the prepared area preceding application of the permanent filler. This approach facilitates optimal contact formation along all edges while minimizing potential issues related to improper positioning often associated with broader cavities lacking sufficient support from individual placements alone. By employing successive wedges strategically within these expansive openings, clinicians can enhance both functional integration post-restoration alongside aesthetic outcomes through enhanced edge definition across treated surfaces exposed directly adjacent each other upon completion.
